title: SkyX
description: SkyX came from one frustration: converting fiat and crypto across multiple tools was slow, fragmented, and unreliable.

content.text: I built SkyX because simple conversion should not require a full browser tab strategy.
The challenge was not the math. It was reliability across different APIs with different failure modes, update frequencies, and limits.
I designed the app as an orchestrator with fallback chains. If one source failed, the system moved to the next and still returned useful output.
I optimized for bad-day behavior, not perfect-day demos.
WebSocket handled real-time crypto updates, while HTTP fallback covered blocked or unstable connections. For historical data, I used caching windows so charts felt instant without hammering APIs.
A converter is only trustworthy when it still works while dependencies are failing.
I also moved third-party calls behind backend routes to avoid CORS chaos and centralize validation, error handling, and request control.
SkyX sharpened how I think about graceful degradation. Real-time is great, but reliability is what users remember.

content.suggestion: Primary API failure with automatic fallback while conversion results continue to update
